attributes.stem: A 21-year-old woman is brought to the Emergency Department with a complaint of unsteadiness on her feet and visual disturbance. She has been unwell with an upper respiratory tract infection for the previous seven days. This was presumed to be viral in origin and treated conservatively. On examination, she is afebrile and haemodynamically stable. There is marked restriction of her lateral gaze bilaterally. The power is preserved in all limbs, but reflexes are not present. Coordination testing shows ataxia. An MRI of the brain is unremarkable. A lumbar puncture demonstrates 4 monocytes [0-4] and an elevated protein of 1.90g/L [0.2-.0.4]
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient with ataxia, ophthalmoplegia, and areflexia most likely has Miller-Fisher syndrome (MFS), a variant of Guillain Barre syndrome (GBS). Just like GBS, it commonly presents 1-4 weeks after a viral upper respiratory tract infection. Whilst the association between viral infections, recent surgery or some vaccinations and resulting GBS is well-known, it is unclear why this occurs. It is assumed to be caused by antibodies against a ganglioside (GQ1b) more commonly found in high levels in cranial nerves III, IV, and VI. Involvement of these cranial nerves leads to rapid-onset ophthalmoplegia. Apart from ophthalmoplegia, the patient may develop ataxia and areflexia. Paralysis of the lower extremities may be present but is less commonly seen as compared to GBS. CSF analysis classically reveals albuminocytologic dissociation (ie, elevated proteins without pleocytosis). Treatment is with plasmapheresis and/or IV immunoglobulins.

A cerebellar lymphoma may present with ataxia but is unlikely to be associated with areflexia and albuminocytologic dissociation. Moreover, a brain MRI would typically reveal a mass lesion.

Multiple sclerosis may present with symptoms similar to MFS. However, a brain MRI would reveal periventricular lesions, and CSF analysis may yield lymphocytic pleocytosis and oligoclonal bands. Albuminocytologic dissociation is unlikely to be seen in multiple sclerosis.

Brainstem involvement would present with hemiparesis, ataxia, nystagmus, dysarthria, and central hypoventilation depending on the location of the brainstem involved. Moreover, patients can have a loss of consciousness or coma due to disruption of the reticular activation system. This can be associated with GQ1b antibodies - the so called Bickerstaff's brainstem encephalitis.
learningPoints: Miller Fisher Syndrome is variant of GBS characterised by cranial nerve involvement.
The triad of signs for Miller Fisher Syndrome are: ophthalmoplegia, ataxia and areflexia.
references: Berlit P, Rakicky J. The Miller Fisher syndrome. Review of the literature. J Clin Neuroophthalmol. 1992 Mar;12(1):57-63. PMID: 1532603.
Teener J. Miller Fisher's Syndrome. Seminars in Neurology. 2013;32(05):512-516.
